content:
14 min read
Just now

--

Note: Please switch to Dark mode for a more immersive experience.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

1.1 Introduction
You like solving equations, right? What, you don’t? Stop lying; we all do!
Right from when we started solving simple ones like 2x + 1 = 17, or moving to high school and facing the quadratic bosses.
Some of you may have even dabbled in some high-degree solving with cubic and quartic equations.
And what about degrees of 5 and higher? Anyone?
Nevertheless, today we are here to discuss a more fundamental method and — I would say — cleverer than all the methods you’ve learnt till now.
This is because not only does it give you the zeros of simple polynomial functions, but so long as your function is well-behaved enough, no matter what it is, the Newton-Raphson method will find your root.
1.2 Use Cases
Let’s discuss where this method would be used in today’s world.
We are doing this before looking at the method itself because I want you to ‘derive’ the concept by sensing the need.
The first is finding the zeros of functions involving terms that we simply cannot solve algebraically
An example of that looks like:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you set this equal to zero, there won't be any clean way to isolate the x.
You might think this kind of situation occurs only in pure mathematics, but that is not at all the case!
For example, consider the famous Kepler’s Equation.
Here, E is an Angular quantity that is present both inside the sine function and algebraically as + E.
For this, no rearrangement can get you the value of E for given M and e.
Thus, organizations like NASA use Newton’s method to find E.
Lastly, not just transcendental equations, Newton's method also helps solve all sorts of high-degree polynomial equations that you could not solve algebraically or even by a brute-force formula (esp. for degrees 5+).
A good example of this would be when finding the rate required to achieve a certain total amount after a given time period from a given principal amount in compound interest.
The formula is:
So clearly, when n gets bigger, it gets harder and harder to find a solution rate; however, Newton's method can do it without any cap on n (it can even be fractional!)
Example of such a situation with A = 10,000 ; P = 2000 and n = 6 will end you up with:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Which is nearly impossible to solve without special methods such as this.
Alright, now that we’ve seen the basic ideas and use-cases associated with this method, let’s discuss the method itself.
And, of course, we shall first build the intuitive understanding before definitions.
However, I’d like to give you a quick historical note before we move on.
The method we will learn here is officially called the Newton-Raphson method and actually has a kind of complicated history.
Isaac Newton’s original 1669 version of this method used absolutely zero calculus.
His original method was purely algebraic.
He would make a guess, substitute a placeholder for the small “error” back into the polynomial, and ignore high-power terms because they were too small and hence get to solve a linear equation for the error. Then he would iterate this, by finding the “error” in the error and so on.
In 1690, Joseph Raphson simplified the method so you don't have to expand large polynomials every time and made it faster and optimised
The elegant, calculus version we will discuss today — using derivatives to find the tangents — was actually published later by Thomas Simpson in 1740.
So, it was finally Simpson who took Newton’s core concept of iterative error correction and used calculus to speed up the algebra.
However, to keep things simple, I’ll be using Newton's name as a placeholder for all the explanations.
1.3 Graphical Derivation
The goal is to find the zeros of a given function. Simple, right?
Like all of calculus, this method was developed by stepping back and thinking about the problem from a bird 's-eye view.
And the best way to get that view on a function is to observe its graph!
So, let's take an example function on which we are gonna do this observation:
This is a cubic whose roots we won't be able to find by any trial and error.
Then here's its graph in the section that we are worried about, i.e., the one with the root:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Right, so let’s begin thinking. Our final goal is to find the x-coordinate of the point marked Z (for zero).
Pause and think how you would approach this for a bit before I reveal the solution.
Done?
Well, here we go.
What Newton observed was that solving linear equations is way easier than any of the crazy ones he was trying to find the solutions to.
So, why not try somehow and bring linear equations into this?
That way, you can solve the simple linear functions and actually get the answer to the original hard problem.
Alright, sounds sweet, but how do we do it?
Well, the next thing he did was approach the toolkit of calculus — that he himself invented —, and specifically, the tool called tangents.
The idea is simple and very similar to how we created the Taylor approximation around chosen points ‘a ’, but we take it further with the power of iterations.
I’ll be more rigorous from now on.
The first step is to pick a reasonably close starting point to your actual root.
We normally also call this the ‘guess’ root, and it doesn't matter what it is as long as it's around the value of your actual root (which we will find).
For our case, we can see in the graph that the root is between 1 and 2, so I’ll take the guess root to be x_0 = 2.
Then the next step is to make a tangent line to the curve at that point.
Of course, this can also be called the linear approximation at x_0 = 2.
I’ve named the x-intercept of the tangent line as A1 (for Approximation 1).
These x-intercepts are the main stars of this method, and the ones that actually approach the answer later (hence the name, Approximation).
From here, we start iteration. The next step is to form the tangent line to the curve at the x-coordinate of point A1.
After the tangent is drawn, name its x-intercept as A2.
As you will see now, this gives us an A2 that is way closer to the actual root Z.
I’ll zoom in a bit so you can see the difference in Z and A2.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

So, in just one iteration, we have gone from our bad guess of 2 to A1, which is 1.6363~, and now A2, which is 1.5303~, which is very close to our actual root.
But we want more accuracy, right? Let's do it one more time!
Take A2’s x-coordinate and draw a tangent to the curve at that point.
Repeating the same steps with x-intercept A3 gives us the new mind-boggling approximation in green here, compared to the pink A2 and the actual solution in blue:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I’ve shown the actual numbers here to showcase the scale; in just two steps of calculations and drawing tangents, we have already found the root correct to four decimal places!
And this method will only get closer and closer to the value. Try doing method 1, 2 more times, and see how you get near 8 decimal places by iteration 4!
Again, note that it feels the same in the visual graph to see the roots of the red function( our cubic) and the blue and black linear functions (tangents).
But with pen and paper, one of them is infinitely easier to solve than the other!
So, with this method, the scary cubics you could never find the roots of become simple problems of running the calculator!
The essence of this method can also be expressed as:
Think of it as finding the root of the linear approximation of the function, which will get us the solution of the original function when done iteratively.
Make sense?
To further our understanding, I’ll briefly mention the algebra that went behind making these tangents, as that's where the actual calculus is happening.
It's pretty simple, see, to make a tangent on a given point on the curve, we simply need two things: 1. The point on the curve itself, and 2. The value of the function's derivative at that point.
Note: The value of the function's derivative at a point is, by definition, the slope of the tangent line at that point.
Then all you have to do is write the line using the point-slope form:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

So, here are the equations of the three lines we drew on the graph, respectively:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

And the numbers in the x — () terms are our approximations x_0, A1, and A2.
The value of y is the value of the function at x equal to those values.
I hope you've realised the reason for applying this method. Finding the x-intercept from these equations is a complete piece of cake!
1.4 Algebraic Representation
Now, as much as I love the drawing-tangents method, it does pose a significant bottleneck in our solving.
We have to first sit and calculate the value of the function at each part, then write the line, find its x-intercept, and then repeat.
While not all, some of these steps can be cut down if we just do some algebra beforehand.
What I mean is that we are performing an algorithm with identical steps, which could be generalised into a formula!
To derive it:
Let's consider A_n to be our current iteration of Newton’s method and A_n+1 the next one.
Thus, by definition, A_n+1 is the x-intercept of the tangent line drawn to the curve — say f(x) — when the x-coordinate is A_n.
We use the same method as last time to get the tangent at the point with A_n as:
Next, we only have to find its x-intercept by putting y = 0:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

And the ‘x ’ we are finding here is simply A_n+1, thus we have the final form of the formula as:
This is equivalent to doing our tangent drawing business, but we can now only focus on this one formula and computing the required values.
One more thing, this is also why this method is so widespread in use! It requires just one differentiation step, one division, and a subtraction!
This simplicity allows it to be easy not only for students in the exam, but more so for the computers doing this kind of solving every single second!
1.5 Picking the starting guess.
Okay, I think you've realised the power of this godsend of a method, let's learn the rules to properly use it, eh? After all, it is unfortunately not omnipotent.
The first question is how we pick the starting guess value.
This is important because if your guess is too far off the actual answer, the method will either take way too many iterations to reach the answer or get stuck in a loop and never get there.
The most powerful method here is, of course, looking at the graph, but algebraically we have a just as strong a tool known as the Intermediate Value Theorem.
The concept of the theorem is pretty simple:
If your continuous function has a positive value at one x and a negative value at another, it has to have taken the value zero at some point in between that interval, i.e., it must have a root in that interval.
The idea of using it is to first try a large range, like from -1 to 1, and see if you can restrict the root there. Then you must pick a dividing value inside that interval and check which half contains the root to narrow your range.
The dividing value is usually just taken to be the midpoint of the interval for simplicity.
Note: This interval “bisection” is also the building block of another numerical root-finding method called the “Bisection Method”
Alright, that's the first part. Next, we can look at some common points to make sure of in Newton’s method itself, so that it converges and gives you a meaningful value:
1. The function must be continuous and differentiable:
The curve cannot have any breaks, jumps, or missing points, and you should be able to take its derivative anywhere near your guess.
2. The derivative must not equal or approach zero:
If the slope becomes flat, the tangent line runs comp
